claim: FAO Food Loss Index estimates 30-40 percent of Sub-Saharan African food is lost postharvest, concentrated at handling, storage, and transport stages, with cereals and perishable crops most affected.

source.title: FAO Food Loss and Waste Database

method: Reviewed FAO Food Loss Index methodology, regional estimates, commodity breakdowns, and loss-stage classification.
limitations: Food-loss data is sparse and modeled for most SSA countries — direct measurement is available in only a few contexts.
Sub-national estimates are not available for most countries — the 30-40 percent figure is a regional aggregate.
Loss-stage classification (handling, storage, transport) depends on survey methodology that varies across countries.
The FAO index combines data from multiple sources with different measurement approaches — cross-country comparison requires explicit harmonization.

claim: APHLIS provides postharvest loss estimates for major cereals at provincial scale across SSA, with losses ranging from 10-30 percent by crop and location, modeled from available survey data and expert assessments.
method: Reviewed APHLIS methodology, loss-estimation models, country coverage, and crop-specific estimates.
limitations: APHLIS covers cereals only — perishable crops (fruits, vegetables, dairy) are not included despite having higher loss rates.
Estimates are modeled from available survey data and expert assessments, not directly measured at provincial scale.
Model inputs vary in quality across countries — some provinces have direct measurement while others rely on regional extrapolation.
APHLIS does not capture loss-stage detail (handling vs. storage vs. transport) at provincial level — aggregate loss estimates mask where in the value chain losses occur.

claim: Cold-chain infrastructure for perishable crops (fruits, vegetables, dairy) is almost absent outside major urban corridors in SSA, making perishable crop losses 30-50 percent higher than cereal losses in most contexts.

source.title: Cold chain infrastructure in Sub-Saharan Africa: a landscape assessment

method: Reviewed FAO cold-chain assessment, refrigeration capacity data, and perishable-crop loss estimates for SSA.
limitations: Cold-chain capacity data is sparse — most SSA countries do not have systematic refrigeration-infrastructure inventories.
The 30-50 percent higher loss figure for perishable crops is a general estimate — actual differences vary by crop, climate, and market distance.
Cold-chain solutions (solar cooling, evaporative cooling) are documented at pilot scale but not systematically assessed for scalability.
The assessment does not capture informal cold-chain practices (shade, water cooling) that may partially reduce losses in some contexts.
